description: Nvidia's 10-K states it utilizes foundries, including TSMC, to produce its semiconductor wafers; TSMC is Nvidia's primary leading-edge foundry.
Nvidia's 10-K states it utilizes foundries, including Samsung Electronics, alongside TSMC, to produce its semiconductor wafers.

Nvidia's 10-K states it purchases memory from SK Hynix Inc.; SK hynix separately announced a June 2026 technology partnership with Nvidia including supply of memory semiconductors for Nvidia's AI infrastructure roadmap (Vera Rubin platforms).
Nvidia's 10-K states it purchases memory from Micron Technology, Inc.
Nvidia's 10-K states it purchases memory from Samsung Electronics, in addition to using Samsung as a wafer foundry.
Nvidia's 10-K names AMD as a current competitor among suppliers of GPUs, custom chips and other accelerated computing solutions.
Nvidia's 10-K names Intel Corporation as a current competitor among suppliers of GPUs, custom chips and other accelerated computing solutions.
Nvidia's 10-K names Broadcom as a competitor in SoC products for automotive, gaming and edge devices.
Nvidia's 10-K names Qualcomm Incorporated as a competitor in SoC products for automotive, gaming and edge devices.
Nvidia's 10-K names Alphabet Inc. among large cloud services companies with internal teams designing hardware/software that compete with Nvidia's accelerated computing platforms.
Nvidia's 10-K names Amazon, Inc. among large cloud services companies with internal teams designing hardware/software that compete with Nvidia's accelerated computing platforms.
Nvidia's 10-K names Microsoft Corporation among large cloud services companies with internal teams designing hardware/software that compete with Nvidia's accelerated computing platforms.

Intel's 10-K discloses that on September 15, 2025, Intel agreed to issue and sell Nvidia 215 million shares of Intel common stock at $23.28/share (about $5.0 billion), completed December 26, 2025, as part of a strategic partnership to co-develop x86 CPU and AI accelerator products.
Nvidia's Grace CPU Superchip, its first Arm-based data center CPU, is built on 144 licensed Arm Neoverse V2 cores.

TSMC (Taiwan) accounted for EUR 8,337.9 million of ASML's total net sales in 2025 (about 25% of EUR 32.7bn total), ASML's largest single-country concentration, driven mainly by TSMC's EUV/DUV lithography purchases.
ASML's 20-F states customers in South Korea, led by Samsung Electronics, represented 25.0% of ASML's 2025 total net sales (EUR 8,159.6 million).

SK hynix committed roughly $7.9 billion (about KRW 12 trillion, covering an estimated 30 EUV scanners) to ASML through 2027 to expand HBM and advanced DRAM production capacity at its M15X and Yongin sites.
Intel's 10-K states ASML is currently the sole supplier of EUV lithography tools that Intel deploys for its Intel 4, Intel 3, Intel 18A and planned future leading-edge manufacturing process nodes.

Arm's 20-F discloses ongoing litigation over Qualcomm's Architecture License Agreement (ALA) with Arm (including the Nuvia ALA dispute), confirming an active Arm architecture license held by Qualcomm.

Apple holds a perpetual Arm architecture license and, per Tom's Hardware, signed a new long-term Arm license agreement extending through 2040, underpinning Apple's custom A-series and M-series silicon.
MediaTek licenses Arm Cortex CPU core designs for its Dimensity smartphone SoC line, an Arm-MediaTek partnership Arm describes on its official partner page.
Samsung's Exynos 2100 mobile SoC is built on Arm's compute and graphics IP, per Arm's official newsroom announcement of the partnership.
Amazon Web Services licenses Arm Neoverse cores for its Graviton server CPU line (Graviton2 through the Neoverse V3-based Graviton5, announced December 2025).
Google Cloud's Axion server CPU is built on licensed Arm Neoverse V2 cores, per Google Cloud's official announcement.
Microsoft Azure's Cobalt 100 server CPU is built on licensed Arm Neoverse N2 cores, per Arm's official newsroom announcement of the partnership.

Samsung Foundry signed a roughly $16.5 billion, multi-year (2025-2033) contract to manufacture Tesla's next-generation AI5/AI6 self-driving chips at its Taylor, Texas fab, confirmed publicly by Elon Musk.
Samsung Foundry began producing camera image sensors (CIS) for Apple iPhones at its Austin S1 fab, breaking Sony's prior exclusive CIS supply relationship with Apple.

South Korea (home to Samsung and SK hynix) represented 20% of Applied Materials' FY2025 net revenue, up 25% year over year; Samsung is among the logic/foundry and memory customers driving Applied Materials' largest segment.
Intel's 10-K states its primary competitor in leading-edge semiconductor process technology is TSMC, which holds a leading position in manufacturing at scale for the most advanced nodes.
Intel's 10-K names AMD as a competitor across the full spectrum of CPUs, GPUs, accelerators and other products.
Intel's 10-K names Qualcomm as a competitor for client computing products.
Intel's 10-K names Nvidia as a competitor in GPU systems and accelerator products, even as the two companies separately entered a co-development and equity partnership in 2025.
Intel's 10-K states Intel competes with Broadcom in the custom ASICs development market.

Taiwan (TSMC's home market) represented 24% of Applied Materials' FY2025 net revenue (up 71% year over year to $6.86 billion), the company's largest single-country jump, reflecting equipment purchases tied to TSMC's capacity expansion.

DRAM-related systems were 26% of Applied Materials' FY2025 Semiconductor Systems segment revenue; SK hynix, alongside Samsung and Micron, is one of the principal DRAM/HBM producers served by this segment.
DRAM and NAND flash memory systems together were 33% of Applied Materials' FY2025 Semiconductor Systems segment revenue; Micron is one of the principal memory producers served by this segment.

Intel's 10-K discloses reliance on a concentrated set of equipment providers for leading-edge process technology; Applied Materials is a top-three equipment supplier to logic/foundry customers including Intel, alongside TSMC and Samsung.
SK hynix's Form F-1 names Samsung Electronics as a major competitor in the DRAM and NAND flash memory markets.
SK hynix's Form F-1 names Micron Technology as a major competitor in the DRAM and NAND flash memory markets.

TSMC's 20-F discloses its ten largest customers accounted for 78% of 2025 net revenue and its single largest customer 19%; independent reporting (CNBC) identifies Apple and Nvidia as TSMC's two largest customers by revenue.
TSMC's 2nm process reached mass production in Q4 2025; reporting identifies Apple, AMD, Qualcomm, MediaTek, Broadcom and Intel among the customers starting mass production or cooperation on the node.
Broadcom is a top TSMC customer for custom AI ASICs; Broadcom publicly flagged that constrained TSMC foundry capacity would limit its chip supply into 2026 amid surging AI accelerator demand.
Qualcomm is among the customers TSMC identified as starting mass production or cooperation on TSMC's 2nm process node in Q4 2025.

Intel is among the customers TSMC identified as starting mass production or cooperation on TSMC's 2nm process node in Q4 2025, illustrating that even IDM/foundry rival Intel relies on TSMC for some leading-edge external manufacturing.
TSMC is fabricating Tesla's AI5 self-driving chip at its Arizona fab, alongside Samsung's parallel production in Texas, per Elon Musk's public confirmation and subsequent reporting.
With TSMC's CoWoS advanced-packaging capacity fully booked by AI GPU demand, TSMC outsourced part of Nvidia's CoWoS packaging (for its Blackwell/Rubin-generation AI GPUs) to ASE Group.
With TSMC's CoWoS advanced-packaging capacity fully booked by AI GPU demand, TSMC outsourced part of Nvidia's CoWoS packaging to US-based Amkor Technology.
TSMC outsourced part of AMD's CoWoS advanced packaging (for its MI355/MI400-series AI accelerators) to ASE/SPIL amid fully booked in-house CoWoS capacity.
